constructive_proposals: abjuration in public before the congregation on a Feast Day
admission to mercy according to Canon ad abolendam
confined in prison for life after abjuration
granting of absolution by Bishops with Apostolic authority
restoration to the sacraments of the Church
sentence of perpetual imprisonment with bread of affliction and water of distress
right to mitigate, aggravate, change, or remit sentence at discretion

scapegoat.name: N.
attributes: convicted by public report and credible persons
infected with heresy for many years to the damage of soul
wounded hearts of judges due to accusation
subject to trial before Bishop or Judge in ecclesiastical and civil authority
scapegoat.justification: N. is positioned as the moral and spiritual transgressor whose actions endanger both Church doctrine and social order; the text frames him as having fallen into grave error, requiring public confession and penance, thus embodying the threat to religious and temporal stability that must be corrected through ritualized reintegration or punishment.

moral_justification: The moral justification centers on protecting the integrity of the Holy Catholic Faith, restoring the sinner to divine order through repentance and ecclesiastical authority, and upholding justice by balancing mercy with punitive measures to prevent scandal and ensure communal holiness.
summary: This passage outlines a detailed ecclesiastical procedure for the public abjuration, confession, and reintegration of an accused heretic (N.) into the Church, blending deconstructive elements—exposing alleged witchcraft as a grave sin and condemning simulated penitence—with constructive mechanisms such as formal confession, ritual reintegration, absolution, and regulated imprisonment. The rhetoric is highly charged with moral urgency, framing the accused as both a spiritual threat and a potential example for others, while maintaining that ultimate authority rests with ecclesiastical judges who may adjust punishment at will.
